content.problemStatement:
You are given a sorted array numsnumsnums of length nnn and an integer xxx. 

Your task is to find the lower bound of xxx in the array.

The **lower bound** of `x` is defined as the **first (smallest) index** `i` such that `nums[i] >= x`.


If no such index exists, return `n` (the size of the array).
The array is sorted in **non-decreasing order**.
Function DeclarationFunction Namesolvesolvesolve – This function finds the lower bound index of a given value in a sorted array.

Parameters
numsnumsnums : A reference to a sorted array of integers.
xxx : The integer value whose lower bound is to be found.
Return Value
Returns the smallest index iii such that nums[i]≥xnums[i] \ge xnums[i]≥x
Returns nnn if no such index exists
Constraints
1≤n≤1051 \leq n \leq 10^51≤n≤105
−105≤nums[i],x≤105-10^5 \leq nums[i], x \leq 10^5−105≤nums[i],x≤105
numsnumsnums is sorted in **non-decreasing (ascending) order**

Input Format

One line containing two integers: nnn and xxx
One line containing nnn space-separated integers — the sorted array


Output Format

Print a single integer — the lower bound index
constraints: nums is sorted in non‑decreasing order
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T (1 ≤ T ≤ 10), the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains two integers n and x (1 ≤ n ≤ 100000, -10^5 ≤ x ≤ 10^5). The second line contains n space‑separated integers nums[0],…,nums[n‑1] sorted in non‑decreasing order.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line with one integer – the smallest 0‑based index i such that nums[i] ≥ x. If no such index exists output n.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The first index with value ≥ 7 is index 3 (0‑based) because nums[3] = 8.
All elements are ≥ -1, so the smallest index is 0.
The value 5 appears at index 4, which is the first position where nums[i] ≥ 5.

hidden.label: edge case – x larger than all elements
general case with negatives and duplicates
x equals first element with many duplicates

solution.approach: For each test case we perform a classic binary search on the sorted array. We keep two pointers low = 0 and high = n. While low < high we examine mid = low + (high - low) / 2. If nums[mid] < x we know the lower bound must be to the right of mid, so we set low = mid + 1; otherwise the candidate is at mid or to its left, so we set high = mid. When the loop finishes low == high and points to the first position where nums[low] ≥ x, or to n if such a position does not exist. This runs in O(log n) time per test case and O(1) extra space.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T · log n) – binary search per test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few integer variables besides the input array.
